departments.title: Sales
departments.desc: How to qualify leads and run new lead intake and outreach — the full Cowboy call model from first contact to the next step.

categories.title: Lead Qualification
categories.desc: How to determine whether a lead is ready to move forward, and what qualifies or disqualifies it.

articles.title: How We Determine if a Lead Is Qualified
articles.body:
A lead is qualified when the customer has a real bail need, we can identify the defendant and bond situation, there is a person willing and able to help, and the lead can reasonably move into the next step of the process.

For Cowboy Bail Bonds, qualification does not mean the bond is approved. Qualification means the lead is worth moving forward into:

intake / cosigner application → payment → cosigner documents → underwriting review → approval decision

Final approval happens later, after payment and cosigner documents are completed and underwriting reviews the bond.

articles.title: Qualification Starts With Discovery
articles.body:
The sales advisor should first connect with the caller and understand the situation before deciding whether the lead is qualified. The call guide's core model is: connect first, work the problem together, guide the next step. It also notes that the guide is not a rigid script and should feel human.

The advisor should determine:

- Who is calling
- Their relationship to the defendant
- Who is in jail
- Where the defendant is being held
- What happened
- Whether the caller is trying to get someone released now or only gathering information
- What matters most to the caller
- Who is available to help
- Whether the caller can move the process forward
- Whether there are obvious obstacles

The goal is to understand intent, urgency, relationship, and ability to help without making the customer feel judged or blocked.

articles.title: A Lead Is Usually Qualified If These Are True
articles.body:
## 1. There Is a Real Bail Need

The lead is qualified if there is an actual defendant in custody, an active bond need, or a likely bond need that Cowboy Bail Bonds can help with.

The advisor should confirm:

- Defendant name
- Jail location
- Bond amount, if known
- Charge or case information, if known
- Whether the customer is trying to move forward now

If the caller is only asking general questions, they may still be a valid lead, but they may be more of an education/nurture lead rather than an immediate conversion lead.

## 2. The Caller Has Intent or Urgency

A stronger lead is someone who wants action, not just information.

Examples of stronger intent:

- “I need to get him out.”
- “What do I need to do?”
- “Can we start today?”
- “How much do I need?”
- “Can you send me the paperwork?”
- “I am ready to pay.”
- “I am the mother/spouse/family member and I need help.”

The guide separates caller types such as information seekers, immediate release customers, payment/resource-constrained customers, overwhelmed customers, comparison shoppers, and complex case customers.

## 3. There Is a Viable Cosigner or Anchor

A qualified lead usually needs someone who can help anchor the bond.

A strong anchor is someone who:

- Can get involved now
- Will stay involved
- Cares about the defendant
- Wants the outcome to be successful
- Can help get things back on track later if needed
- Is reachable and responsive

Examples of stronger anchors:

- Parent
- Spouse
- Long-term partner
- Sibling
- Close family member
- Employer or trusted support person
- Someone with stable contact information and willingness to sign

Examples of weaker anchors:

- New friend
- Casual acquaintance
- Someone who barely knows the defendant
- Someone who refuses responsibility
- Someone who is hard to reach
- Someone who is only calling for information but cannot make decisions

If the caller is not the right person, the lead may still be qualified if they can bring in the right person.

## 4. There Is Some Ability to Pay

A lead does not have to be perfect financially to be qualified, but there needs to be a realistic path to payment.

The advisor should understand:

- How much money is available today
- Whether other family members can help
- Whether a payment plan may be possible
- Whether the customer understands that payment comes before underwriting review
- Whether the customer is willing to move forward if terms are workable

Money should be handled carefully. The guide says not to let money become the first wall between Cowboy and the customer, and not to introduce down payment unless the customer brings it up, it becomes necessary, or the advisor is moving them into the next step.

## 5. There Are No Immediate Stop Signs

Some leads require deeper review or escalation before moving forward.

Possible warning signs:

- Very high bond
- Serious charge
- Long criminal history
- Weak or unavailable cosigner
- Caller is not the decision maker
- Caller has no money and no support system
- Defendant has a history of missing court
- Customer is unwilling to provide basic information
- Customer is hostile, dishonest, or evasive
- The bond appears outside normal company risk tolerance

The call guide says not to overqualify too early. The goal is to get to the next step unless there is a major obstacle, high bond, or obvious risk signal that requires deeper discovery.

articles.title: Lead Qualification Categories
articles.body:
## Qualified / Ready to Move Forward

This lead should move into intake, payment, and cosigner documents.

Typical signs:

- Real defendant and bond need
- Caller wants action now
- Viable cosigner or anchor
- Payment ability or realistic payment path
- Customer is responsive
- No obvious disqualifying risk

Next step: Start intake / cosigner application and move toward payment.

## Qualified but Needs Another Person

This lead may be good, but the current caller is not enough.

Typical signs:

- Caller cares but cannot sign
- Caller does not have money
- Caller is not the decision maker
- Another family member needs to be involved
- A stronger cosigner is needed

Next step: Bring in the parent, spouse, family member, employer, or stronger support person.

## Qualified but Needs Escalation

This lead may be valuable but needs manager or underwriting input before moving too far.

Typical signs:

- High bond
- Serious charge
- Unclear risk
- Weak cosigner
- Complicated court situation
- Customer has partial money but not enough clarity
- Staff is unsure whether Cowboy should proceed

Next step: Escalate internally before making promises.

## Not Ready / Nurture Lead

This lead is not ready to move forward yet, but should still be documented and followed up with.

Typical signs:

- Caller is only gathering information
- No bond amount yet
- Defendant may not be booked yet
- Customer needs to speak with family
- Customer is shopping around
- Customer does not know who can sign or pay

Next step: Educate, document, follow up, and keep the door open.

## Not Qualified

This lead is not workable as currently presented.

Typical signs:

- No real bail need
- No defendant information
- No available cosigner or support
- No payment path
- Customer refuses basic requirements
- Risk is clearly outside company tolerance
- Customer cannot move the process forward and cannot connect us to someone who can

Next step: Document the reason and close or pause the lead based on company process.

articles.title: Qualification Questions Staff Should Ask
articles.body:
Staff should ask enough to understand the situation without making the call feel like an interrogation.

Key questions:

- Who am I speaking with?
- What is your relationship to the defendant?
- What happened?
- Where are they being held?
- Are you trying to get them released now, or are you just looking for information?
- What is the most important thing to you right now?
- Do you know the bond amount?
- Who is able to cosign?
- Besides you, is anyone else involved in helping?
- How much are you able to put down today?
- Are you ready to start the paperwork if we can help?
- Is there anything that may make this situation harder, such as prior missed court, serious charges, or lack of support?

The guide specifically recommends understanding what is good, what is bad, what obstacles may exist, what resources are available, who is willing to help, and whether the caller can anchor the bond.

categories.title: New Lead Intake & Outreach
categories.desc: How to run the first conversation, follow the call model, and hand off a new lead correctly.

articles.title: New Lead Intake and Outreach
articles.body:
When a new lead comes in, the goal is to respond quickly, make the customer feel guided, understand the situation, and move the lead into the correct next step.

A new lead may come from a phone call, website form, online bail flow, Google Business Profile, walk-in, referral, attorney, past customer, or Spanish-speaking inquiry.

The sales approach should follow Cowboy's call model: connect first, work the problem together, and guide the next step. The call guide is not meant to be a rigid script. It should sound human and natural, using Cowboy language.

The main goal at this stage is to turn the customer's stress and confusion into a clear plan.

articles.title: What Happens First
articles.body:
When a new lead comes in, the first person handling the lead should slow the conversation down, thank the caller, use a human tone, and invite them to explain the situation. The caller should feel like Cowboy Bail Bonds is with them before we start quoting, solving, or collecting paperwork.

The first conversation should identify:

- Who is calling
- Their relationship to the defendant
- Who is in jail
- Where the defendant is being held
- What happened
- Whether they are looking for information or trying to get someone released
- What matters most to them right now
- Whether they are ready to move forward
- Whether they can help anchor the bond
- Whether anyone else needs to be involved
- Whether the customer needs Spanish support
- Whether there are obvious obstacles, such as lack of funds, limited family support, serious charges, or a caller who is not the decision maker

The caller should not feel judged, blocked, rushed, or treated like a checklist. The advisor should understand the situation before pushing price, paperwork, or payment.

articles.title: Recommended Call Flow
articles.body:
The call should generally follow this structure:

1. Thank them and invite the situation.
2. Find out who is calling and what they need.
3. Ask what matters most outside of getting the person out.
4. Work the problem together by understanding the good, the bad, the obstacles, the resources, and the support available.
5. Evaluate whether the caller can help move things forward.
6. Handle price only when it comes up or when it becomes necessary.
7. Guide the next step.
8. Set expectations.
9. Confirm who does what next.

The customer should be told clearly what happens next:

intake / cosigner application → payment → cosigner documents → underwriting review → approval decision → bond posting → release → defendant application and documents after release

articles.title: Stakeholders Responsible
articles.body:
## Sales Advisor / Customer Care / Bond Specialist

This is the first owner of the lead.

They are responsible for:

- Answering inbound calls
- Calling back missed calls or online leads
- Responding to new website or online bail submissions
- Thanking the caller and creating a calm first impression
- Understanding who is calling and what they need
- Identifying whether the caller is an information seeker, immediate release customer, price shopper, overwhelmed customer, complex case customer, or resource-constrained customer
- Asking discovery questions before jumping to price
- Explaining the process
- Collecting basic defendant and cosigner information
- Qualifying the lead without overqualifying too early
- Moving workable leads toward intake, payment, and cosigner documents
- Documenting the conversation in ePros

The advisor should not make the caller feel like a burden, should not jump straight to price, and should not sound like they are only asking for money.

## Closer / Bond Closer

The closer becomes more involved once the customer is ready to move forward.

They are responsible for:

- Making sure the intake and cosigner application are complete
- Explaining payment requirements
- Sending payment links or coordinating in-office payment
- Sending cosigner documents
- Confirming signatures, IDs, references, and required information
- Keeping the customer moving toward underwriting review
- Making sure the customer understands that payment and cosigner documents come before the underwriting decision

## Underwriting Team / Manager

Underwriting gets involved after intake, payment, and cosigner documents are complete.

They are responsible for:

- Reviewing the defendant, bond, cosigner, payment, references, and risk factors
- Evaluating whether the caller or cosigner is a strong anchor
- Making the approval decision
- Requesting more information if needed
- Requesting a stronger cosigner or collateral if needed
- Declining the bond if it does not meet company requirements
- Documenting the decision clearly

A strong anchor is someone who can get involved now, stay involved, cares about the defendant, wants a successful outcome, can help keep things on track later, and is reachable and responsive.

## Shift Manager / Sales Manager

The manager is responsible for oversight.

They should monitor:

- Missed calls
- Open leads
- Online bail submissions
- Leads waiting on payment
- Leads waiting on cosigner documents
- Leads waiting on underwriting
- Staff follow-up quality
- Handoffs between shifts
- Lost lead reasons
- Whether staff are following the Cowboy call model
- Whether customers are being guided clearly into the next step

## Posting / Court Team

After approval, the posting or court-side team is responsible for making sure the bond is prepared and posted correctly.

They are responsible for:

- Verifying jail and court details
- Preparing the bond
- Posting the bond
- Updating the case status
- Communicating bond posted confirmation
- Setting expectations around release timing
- Making sure the customer understands that defendant application and defendant documents happen after release

articles.title: Automations That May Occur
articles.body:
## Phone and Lead Capture Automations

Depending on the lead source, the system may capture:

- Inbound call tracking
- Missed call notifications
- Missed call text-back
- Website form submission
- Online bail submission
- Google Business Profile call tracking
- Internal notifications to staff or managers

The purpose of these automations is to make sure no lead is missed and every customer gets a fast response.

## Online Bail / eBail Automations

For online bail leads, the customer may be guided through a digital flow that includes:

- Online intake
- Progress bar
- Photo ID upload
- Selfie upload
- Cosigner information
- Payment step
- Status updates in ePros
- Staff notification that a customer has started or completed a step

The online process should make it easy for staff to see where the customer is in the journey and what is still missing.

## ePros Workflow Automations

Inside ePros, staff may use statuses, buttons, and workflows to move the lead forward.

Examples include:

- Creating or updating the case
- Updating lead or case status
- Sending SMS messages
- Sending payment links
- Sending cosigner documents
- Sending documents to Cosigner 1, Cosigner 2, or Cosigner 3
- Tracking document completion
- Tracking whether the lead is waiting on payment, documents, underwriting, approval, or posting

The goal is to make the workflow visible so managers and staff can quickly tell where each lead stands.

## SMS / Follow-Up Automations

Text messages may be used to:

- Confirm receipt of the inquiry
- Send the application link
- Send payment links
- Send document links
- Remind cosigners to complete documents
- Tell the customer what is missing
- Confirm when the bond has been posted
- Explain next steps after release

These messages should support staff communication, not replace it entirely. In high-value or urgent situations, a phone call is still important.

articles.title: Documentation and Scripts Used
articles.body:
## Sales Agent Call Outline / Guide

The primary call documentation is the Sales Agent Call Outline / Guide.

The guide is built around three principles: Connect first. Work the problem together. Guide the next step.

It reminds advisors that customers should feel guided before they feel quoted, and that Cowboy sells by understanding the customer, collaborating in discovery, and guiding the next step.

## First Call Script / Opening Language

Staff should use a consistent first-call structure, but not sound robotic.

Optional opening language includes:

- “Thank you for calling. Let's talk through what's going on and see how we can help.”
- “Well, let's talk through this a little bit. Let me know the situation and how I can help you here.”

The call should cover:

- Greeting
- Reassurance
- Defendant name and jail location
- Caller relationship to defendant
- What happened
- Whether they are trying to get someone released or just gathering information
- What matters most right now
- Who can help
- Clear next step

## Discovery Questions

The call guide recommends early discovery questions such as:

- Who am I speaking with?
- What is your relationship to the defendant?
- What happened, and where are things right now?
- Are you mainly looking for information, or are you trying to get someone released?
- What is the most important thing to you right now?

A key question to help understand the customer's real concern is: “Outside of getting them out, what is your number one concern right now?”

## Qualifying Checklist

Staff should have a checklist for the minimum information needed to move the lead forward. This should include:

- Defendant name
- Date of birth, if available
- Jail location
- Bond amount
- Charge information, if available
- Caller name and phone number
- Caller relationship to defendant
- Cosigner name and phone number
- Whether the caller can anchor the bond
- Payment ability
- References
- Language preference
- Notes about urgency or risk

The call guide also reminds staff not to overqualify too early. The goal is usually to get to the next step unless there is a major obstacle, high bond, or obvious risk signal that requires deeper discovery.

## Money / Price Handling Script

Money should be handled clearly, but it should not control the call.

The guide says the goal is to acknowledge money without letting it become the first wall between Cowboy and the customer. Down payment should not be introduced unless the customer brings it up, it naturally becomes necessary, or the advisor is moving the customer into the actual next step.

Optional language: “Money matters, and we will talk through it clearly. First, let me understand the situation so I can tell you what options actually make sense.”

## Next-Step Script

Once the advisor understands the situation, they should guide the customer into the next step.

The guide recommends thanking the caller, reflecting what matters most, showing care, connecting the next step to the situation, using Cowboy's experience naturally, moving workable leads toward paperwork, and setting expectations.

Optional language: “Thank you for walking me through that. I know this is a lot, and we are going to help you through it. Based on what you told me, I think the best next step is to get the paperwork started so we can begin moving this forward. I will walk you through what we need and what happens next.”

articles.title: Next-Step Paths
articles.body:
After discovery, staff should choose the right next-step path.

The possible paths are:

- Move this caller forward
- Get them in the door
- Bring in another person
- Educate and nurture
- Escalate internally

Most of the time, “get them in the door” means starting paperwork, moving toward commitment, and discussing payment only when the customer brings it up or when it becomes necessary.

articles.title: Handoff, Expectations & Ownership Close
articles.body:
## Handoff and Expectation Script

Before handing the customer to the next person or next step, the advisor should explain:

- What Cowboy will do next
- What the customer needs to do next
- What information may still be needed
- What happens after paperwork starts
- Who will be involved next
- What timing to expect

The guide specifically says to avoid overpromising, making underwriting sound like a surprise, creating compliance fear on the sales call, or going too deep into underwriting process design.

## Ownership Close

Every lead conversation should end with ownership.

The caller should know:

- The next action
- Who owns it
- Timing
- What happens if something changes

Optional closing language: “Here is what we are going to do next. I am going to get this started, and I will make sure you know what we need from you. After that, we will walk you through the next part so you know what to expect.”

## Handoff Notes

Every lead should have clear notes so another staff member can pick up the file without making the customer start over.

Notes should include:

- Who called
- Their relationship to the defendant
- What they need
- What matters most to them
- What was quoted or explained
- What information was collected
- What is missing
- Whether payment was sent or received
- Whether cosigner documents were sent or completed
- Whether the file is ready for underwriting
- Next follow-up time
- Staff member responsible
- Any risk factors, obstacles, or escalation notes

departments.title: Customer Journey
departments.desc: The full Cowboy Bail Bonds customer journey from first need for bail help through final case resolution.

categories.title: Overview
categories.desc: How the full customer journey fits together, from first contact through case resolution.

articles.title: Purpose & Overview
articles.body:
This document maps the full Cowboy Bail Bonds customer journey from the moment someone needs bail help through the final resolution of the case.

The goal is to clearly understand each step of the customer experience, where customers may feel confused or stressed, where staff need consistent process, and how Cowboy Bail Bonds can create a faster, clearer, more professional experience from start to finish.

## Simple Customer Journey

Find us → Contact us → Intake / cosigner application → Pay → Cosigner signs documents → Underwriting review → Approval decision → Bond is posted → Defendant is released → Defendant completes application and documents → Court, check-ins, payments → Case is closed

categories.title: Pre-Sale & Intake
categories.desc: Awareness through cosigner documents — the first half of the customer's path to a posted bond.

articles.title: 1. Awareness / Search
articles.body:
The customer is usually a family member, friend, spouse, or defendant who needs immediate help after an arrest.

They may find Cowboy Bail Bonds through:

- Google search
- Google Business Profile
- Website
- Local jail pages
- Referrals
- Attorneys
- Past customers
- Word of mouth
- Spanish or bilingual searches
- Physical office visibility

## Customer mindset

The customer is stressed, confused, emotional, and usually in a hurry. They may not understand the bail process, what it costs, how long release takes, or who they can trust.

## What matters most

Cowboy Bail Bonds needs to appear trustworthy, fast, experienced, local, and easy to work with before the customer ever calls.

articles.title: 2. First Contact / Lead Capture
articles.body:
The customer contacts Cowboy Bail Bonds by phone, website form, online bail flow, or walk-in.

At this stage, the basic information needs to be collected quickly:

- Who is in jail?
- Where are they being held?
- What is the bond amount?
- Who is calling?
- Who can cosign?
- How quickly do they need help?
- How much money do they have available?
- Are they ready to move forward?

## Customer mindset

They want answers quickly. They want to know the price, the process, how long it takes, and whether Cowboy Bail Bonds can help.

## What matters most

Calls need to be answered quickly, staff need to sound calm and confident, and the customer needs to feel like they are being guided through the process.

articles.title: 3. Intake / Cosigner Application
articles.body:
Once the lead is qualified and ready to move forward, Cowboy Bail Bonds collects the intake information and cosigner application information needed to begin the process.

Information collected may include:

- Defendant information
- Cosigner information
- Caller information
- Employment
- Address history
- References
- Case and jail details
- Bond amount
- Charge information
- Payment ability
- Risk factors
- Contact information
- Language preference, if applicable

This information goes into ePros and becomes the foundation for payment, cosigner documents, underwriting review, approval decision, bond posting, court reminders, check-ins, and follow-up.

## Customer mindset

They may feel overwhelmed by the amount of information needed. They may not understand why certain details are required, especially if they are stressed and trying to move quickly.

## What matters most

The intake and cosigner application process should feel simple, guided, and organized. The customer should know what information is needed, why it matters, and what happens next.

articles.title: 4. Payment Collection
articles.body:
After intake and the cosigner application information are collected, Cowboy Bail Bonds collects the required payment before the underwriting review and approval decision.

This stage may include:

- Down payment
- Payment link
- In-office payment
- Payment plan setup, if applicable
- Subscription or recurring payment setup, if applicable
- Explanation of future due dates
- Explanation of late fees or missed payment consequences
- Confirmation that payment was received

## Customer mindset

The customer wants to move quickly and may believe that making payment means the bond is automatically approved or immediately ready to post. They may also be under financial pressure and need the terms explained clearly.

## What matters most

Staff need to clearly explain what the payment covers, what is due now, what may be due later, and that the bond still must go through cosigner documents, underwriting review, and approval before the bond is posted.

The customer should understand:

- Intake and cosigner application must be completed
- Payment must be received
- Cosigner documents must be signed
- Underwriting must review the bond
- Approval decision comes after review
- Bond posting happens after approval

articles.title: 5. Cosigner Documents
articles.body:
After payment is collected, the cosigner completes the required documents. These documents are needed before Cowboy Bail Bonds can make the underwriting decision.

This may include:

- Cosigner documents
- Promissory note
- Indemnity agreement
- Reference forms
- Payment agreement
- Bond conditions, if applicable
- ID or selfie upload
- Additional cosigner packets, if needed

The current ePros and online flow includes statuses and buttons for sending documents to Cosigner 1, Cosigner 2, and Cosigner 3.

## Customer mindset

The customer wants the paperwork done quickly so the bond can move forward. Cosigners may hesitate if responsibilities are unclear or if they do not understand what they are signing.

## What matters most

The cosigner document process needs to be easy to complete, easy to explain, and easy for staff to track. Missing signatures, missing IDs, unclear responsibilities, or stalled cosigner packets delay the underwriting decision.

categories.title: Underwriting & Release
categories.desc: Risk review, approval, bond posting, and the defendant's release.

articles.title: 6. Underwriting / Risk Review
articles.body:
After intake, payment, and cosigner documents are complete, the team reviews the bond and determines whether Cowboy Bail Bonds can approve it.

Factors may include:

- Bond amount
- Charge type
- Criminal history
- Court history
- Defendant stability
- Cosigner strength
- Cosigner documents
- Employment
- References
- Payment ability
- Risk level
- Court or bond conditions
- Collateral, if needed
- Any special circumstances that affect risk

## Customer mindset

The customer has already provided information, made payment, and completed cosigner documents, so they are expecting a fast answer. They may be nervous about whether the bond will be approved, whether more information is needed, whether a stronger cosigner is needed, or whether collateral is required.

## What matters most

The underwriting review needs to be fast, consistent, and clearly communicated internally. The customer should feel like Cowboy Bail Bonds is working toward a solution and not leaving them in the dark.

articles.title: 7. Approval Decision / Next Steps
articles.body:
After underwriting review, Cowboy Bail Bonds makes the approval decision and communicates the outcome to the customer.

Possible outcomes include:

- Approved
- Approved with additional requirements
- Needs stronger cosigner
- Needs more information
- Needs collateral
- Declined

## If approved, staff explain:

- The bond is approved
- What happens before the bond is posted
- What happens after release
- What responsibilities the defendant and cosigner have
- What the defendant must complete after release
- What court, check-in, and payment expectations will apply

## If additional requirements are needed, staff explain:

- What is missing
- Why it is needed
- Who needs to provide it
- How quickly it needs to be completed

## If declined, staff explain:

- That Cowboy Bail Bonds cannot move forward as submitted
- Whether another cosigner, collateral, or more information could change the decision
- What happens with any payment already collected, based on company policy

## Customer mindset

The customer wants a direct answer. They do not want vague language or delays. They need to know whether the bond is moving forward and what exact step comes next.

## What matters most

The approval communication should be clear, direct, and action-oriented. The customer should never be left wondering where they stand.

articles.title: 8. Bond Posting
articles.body:
After approval, Cowboy Bail Bonds posts the bond.

This stage includes:

- Verifying jail and court details
- Preparing the bond
- Posting the bond
- Updating internal case status
- Communicating expected release timing
- Notifying the customer that the bond has been posted

## Customer mindset

They expect immediate action once they have completed intake, paid, signed cosigner documents, and received approval. They want to know when their loved one will be released.

## What matters most

The customer needs confirmation and realistic expectations. Even when jail release times are outside Cowboy's control, the customer should feel informed.

articles.title: 9. Release
articles.body:
After the bond is posted, the defendant is released from jail.

This stage includes:

- Monitoring release status
- Communicating release expectations
- Confirming when the defendant is out, when possible
- Preparing the customer and defendant for the next steps
- Explaining that the defendant must complete their own application and documents after release

## Customer mindset

The customer is relieved but may still be anxious about timing. They may think the process is complete once the defendant is out.

## What matters most

The customer and defendant need to understand that release is not the end of the process. After release, the defendant still needs to complete their application, sign documents, and understand their responsibilities.

categories.title: Post-Release & Compliance
categories.desc: What happens after release — onboarding, court reminders, check-ins, and ongoing compliance.

articles.title: 10. Defendant Application / Defendant Documents
articles.body:
After the defendant is released, Cowboy Bail Bonds collects the defendant's application and required documents.

This may include:

- Defendant application
- Defendant documents
- Defendant ID or selfie upload
- Address and contact confirmation
- Employment information
- Court date acknowledgment
- Check-in requirements
- Bond condition acknowledgment
- Payment responsibility review, if applicable
- Explanation of what happens if the defendant misses court
- Explanation of what happens if the defendant misses check-ins
- Explanation of what happens if payments are missed

## Customer mindset

The defendant may feel relieved to be out and may not fully understand that they now have serious responsibilities. They may be tired, distracted, or trying to move on quickly.

## What matters most

The defendant needs a clear post-release onboarding process. They should understand their court dates, check-ins, payment obligations, bond conditions, and communication requirements before problems happen.

articles.title: 11. Post-Release Onboarding
articles.body:
After the defendant completes their application and documents, the customer, cosigner, and defendant need clear instructions for the life of the bond.

This stage includes:

- Court date reminders
- Check-in requirements
- NotifiCourt setup
- Payment obligations
- Bond conditions
- Contact expectations
- What to do if a court date changes
- What happens if the defendant misses court
- Who to contact for help

## Customer mindset

The customer may think the hardest part is over once the defendant is released. In reality, the compliance period is just beginning.

## What matters most

The defendant and cosigner need to clearly understand their responsibilities from the beginning. Good post-release communication helps prevent missed court dates, missed check-ins, payment issues, and confusion.

articles.title: 12. Ongoing Service / Compliance
articles.body:
During the life of the bond, Cowboy Bail Bonds manages the relationship with the defendant and cosigner.

Ongoing touchpoints include:

- Court reminders
- Check-ins
- Payment reminders
- Missed payment follow-up
- Court date updates
- Bond condition tracking
- Address and phone number updates
- Customer service issues
- Risk monitoring

Systems and processes may include:

- ePros
- NotifiCourt
- SMS reminders
- Staff follow-up
- Court event tracking
- Payment plan monitoring
- Collections dashboards

## Customer mindset

The customer may be busy, confused, avoidant, or unaware of how serious missed court, missed check-ins, or missed payments can become.

## What matters most

Communication needs to be proactive and consistent. The goal is to prevent problems before they become emergencies.

articles.title: 13. Collections / Problem Resolution
articles.body:
If the customer falls behind on payments or stops communicating, the account may move into collections or escalation.

This stage includes:

- Past due payment follow-up
- Defendant contact
- Cosigner contact
- Reference contact
- Payment arrangements
- Risk-based escalation
- First past due date tracking
- Total past due tracking
- Collector accountability
- Documentation of collection activity

## Customer mindset

They may be avoiding calls because they cannot pay, are embarrassed, are frustrated, or do not understand the consequences.

## What matters most

The collections process needs to be firm, organized, consistent, and solution-focused. Early intervention gives Cowboy Bail Bonds the best chance of resolving the issue before the balance becomes harder to collect.

articles.title: 14. Case Closeout
articles.body:
At the end of the case, the bond is resolved.

Possible outcomes include:

- Case completed
- Bond discharged or exonerated
- Refund processed, if applicable
- Account paid in full
- Account closed
- Account written off or escalated
- Customer retained for future referral

## Customer mindset

They want the matter finished and may need help understanding refunds, balances, or final obligations.

## What matters most

The case should be closed cleanly. The customer should understand the final status, whether anything is still owed, and whether their obligations are complete.

categories.title: Reference & Summary
categories.desc: Cross-cutting reference material: every touchpoint and the moments that matter most.

articles.title: Major Customer Touchpoints
articles.body:
The main customer touchpoints are:

- Google search
- Google Business Profile
- Website
- Phone call
- Office visit
- Online bail application
- Text messages
- ePros workflow
- Payment links
- Cosigner document signing
- Bond posted notification
- Release communication
- Defendant application after release
- Defendant document signing after release
- NotifiCourt check-ins
- Court reminders
- Collections calls and texts
- Final closeout communication

articles.title: Key Moments That Matter
articles.body:
## 1. First Phone Call

This is where trust is either created or lost. The customer needs speed, confidence, clarity, and empathy.

## 2. Intake and Cosigner Application

The customer needs a clear process and should not feel like they are answering the same questions repeatedly.

## 3. Payment Collection

The customer needs clear payment terms, a simple way to pay, and an understanding that payment is part of moving the bond forward.

## 4. Cosigner Documents

Cosigner documents must be completed before underwriting can make a decision. If documents are confusing or cosigners hesitate, the bond can stall.

## 5. Underwriting Review

The team needs to review the bond quickly and consistently after intake, payment, and cosigner documents are complete.

## 6. Approval Decision

The customer needs to understand whether Cowboy Bail Bonds can move forward, whether additional requirements are needed, or whether the bond is declined.

## 7. Bond Posted Confirmation

After approval, the customer needs reassurance that Cowboy has acted and that the release process is moving forward.

## 8. Release Communication

The customer needs realistic expectations around jail release timing and what happens after the defendant is out.

## 9. Defendant Application and Documents

After release, the defendant must complete their application and documents. This step is critical because the defendant now needs to fully understand their obligations.

## 10. Post-Release Instructions

The defendant and cosigner need to understand court dates, check-ins, payment obligations, and bond conditions.

## 11. Ongoing Compliance

Consistent reminders and follow-up help prevent missed court dates, missed payments, and avoidable escalations.

## 12. Collections

When payment problems happen, the process needs to be structured, documented, and consistent.

## 13. Case Closeout

The customer should understand when the case is finished, what remains, and whether any refund or balance applies.

departments.title: ePros Sales Stages
departments.desc: The ePros sales stages reflecting the real path a lead takes from first contact through booking, follow-up, application, review, approval, posting, defendant follow-up, and closeout.

categories.title: Overview
categories.desc: How the ePros stage order reflects the real path a lead takes.

articles.title: Overview & Core Stage Order
articles.body:
The ePros sales stages should reflect the real path a lead takes from first contact through booking status, follow-up, application, review, approval, posting, defendant follow-up, and final closeout.

Sales staff should still follow the Cowboy call model during the early stages: connect first, work the problem together, and guide the next step. The call guide is meant to sound human, not like a rigid script.

categories.title: Early Stage (Intake & Follow-Up)
categories.desc: The stages a lead moves through before it's ready for an application.

articles.title: 1. Intake
articles.body:
This is the first internal stage when a new lead comes in.

The goal is to understand the situation, identify the defendant, understand who is calling, and determine what needs to happen next.

This stage includes:

- New inbound call
- Website lead
- Online bail lead
- Walk-in
- Referral
- Google Business Profile call
- Past customer inquiry
- Spanish-speaking inquiry

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Customer Care / Bond Specialist

Goal: Capture the lead and start the process.

articles.title: 2. JINFO — Jail Info / New Lead
articles.body:
This stage is used when staff are collecting or confirming jail information.

This may include:

- Defendant name
- Date of birth, if available
- Jail location
- Booking status
- Bond amount
- Charge information
- Court or county information
- Caller relationship to defendant
- Whether the customer is trying to move forward now

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Customer Care

Goal: Confirm the basic defendant and jail information so the lead can move forward.

articles.title: 3. NYA / NFB — Not Yet Arraigned / Not Fully Booked
articles.body:
This stage is used when the defendant is not ready for a bond yet because they have not been fully processed.

NYA means Not Yet Arraigned. NFB means Not Fully Booked.

This may apply when:

- The defendant is still being booked
- No bond has been set yet
- The defendant has not been arraigned
- Jail information is incomplete
- The case is too early to move forward
- Staff need to monitor and follow up later

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Customer Care

Goal: Keep the lead alive while waiting for the defendant to become bondable.

articles.title: 4. FUP — Follow-Up
articles.body:
This stage is used when the lead needs additional follow-up before it can move forward.

This may include:

- Waiting on the customer to call back
- Waiting on jail information
- Waiting on bond amount
- Waiting on family decision
- Waiting on payment discussion
- Waiting on a possible cosigner
- Waiting on another decision maker
- Following up with a price shopper
- Following up with an online lead that did not finish

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Customer Care

Goal: Prevent the lead from going cold and move it into the next actionable stage.

articles.title: 5. APPSC — Appointment Scheduled
articles.body:
This stage is used when the customer has scheduled an appointment or committed to a next step.

This may include:

- Office appointment scheduled
- Phone appointment scheduled
- Customer coming in
- Cosigner coming in
- Customer planning to complete the next step at a set time
- Staff waiting for the customer to arrive or reconnect

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Closer

Goal: Convert interest into action by getting the customer to the next scheduled step.

categories.title: Application & Underwriting
categories.desc: Cosigner application, manager review, and the underwriting decision.

articles.title: 6. CSAPS — Cosigner Application
articles.body:
This stage is used when the cosigner application is being completed.

This is a major pre-review stage because the cosigner information helps Cowboy determine whether the bond can move forward.

This may include:

- Cosigner personal information
- Relationship to defendant
- Employment
- Address history
- Phone number
- References
- Payment ability
- Stability
- Willingness to be responsible
- Any issues that may affect risk

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Closer

Goal: Complete the cosigner application so the file can move into review.

## Online Process Replacement for CSAPS

For online leads, the online process can take the place of the standard CSAPS — Cosigner Application stage.

Instead of manually moving through the normal cosigner application process, the lead may move through:

1. OLPAY — Payment
2. OLSGN / OLSGN1 / OLSGN2 / OLSGN3 — Cosigner Documents

These online stages replace the standard cosigner application stage when the customer is completing the process digitally.

## OLPAY — Payment

This is the online payment stage.

This may include:

- Payment link sent
- Down payment collected
- Payment plan started
- Payment confirmed
- Customer moved forward in the online flow

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Closer

Goal: Collect the required payment so the online lead can continue forward.

Important note: Payment does not mean the bond is approved yet. Payment is part of moving the lead forward before review and approval.

## OLSGN / OLSGN1 / OLSGN2 / OLSGN3 — Cosigner Documents

This is the online signing stage for cosigner documents.

This may include:

- Cosigner documents sent
- Cosigner documents signed
- ID or selfie uploaded
- Promissory note completed
- Indemnity agreement completed
- Payment agreement completed
- Additional cosigner packets completed

Stage breakdown:

- OLSGN / OLSGN1 — Cosigner 1 documents
- OLSGN2 — Cosigner 2 documents
- OLSGN3 — Cosigner 3 documents

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Closer

Goal: Get the required cosigner documents completed so the file can move into manager review and underwriting.

articles.title: 7. MGRW — Floor Manager Review of Application
articles.body:
This stage is used when the application is ready for a floor manager to review before underwriting.

The manager should review:

- Application completeness
- Cosigner information
- Payment status
- Documents completed
- Missing information
- Obvious risk issues
- Whether the file is ready for underwriting
- Whether more information is needed before review

Main owner: Floor Manager / Shift Manager

Goal: Make sure the file is clean and ready before underwriting reviews it.

articles.title: 8. Underwriting Review
articles.body:
This is the formal review stage where Cowboy evaluates the risk and determines whether the bond can be approved.

Underwriting may review:

- Bond amount
- Charge type
- Defendant history
- Court history
- Defendant stability
- Cosigner strength
- Payment collected
- References
- Employment
- Residence stability
- Prior failures to appear
- Existing obligations
- Collateral, if needed
- Any special risk factors

Main owner: Underwriting / Manager

Goal: Decide whether Cowboy can approve the bond, needs more information, needs more money, needs another cosigner, or should decline.

articles.title: 9. NEEDS — Needs Info / Needs Action
articles.body:
This stage is used when underwriting or management cannot approve the file as-is.

This may include:

- Need more information
- Need additional money
- Need stronger cosigner
- Need second or third cosigner
- Need collateral
- Need missing documents
- Need missing ID
- Need updated references
- Need manager clarification
- Need customer to resolve an issue before approval

Main owner: Sales Advisor / Closer / Manager

Goal: Resolve whatever is missing so the file can return to review or move to approval.

This stage may loop backward to:

- Follow-up
- Cosigner application
- Online payment
- Online signing
- Manager review
- Underwriting review

categories.title: Approval & Posting
categories.desc: What happens once a bond is approved and cleared to post.

articles.title: 10. OLAPP / APRVD — Approved
articles.body:
This stage means the bond has been approved.

OLAPP can be used for online approval. APRVD can be used for general approved status.

This means the file has cleared review and Cowboy is ready to move toward posting.

This stage may include:

- Approval confirmed
- Customer notified
- Any final conditions confirmed
- File moved toward posting
- Posting team prepared

Main owner: Underwriting / Manager

Goal: Mark the bond approved and ready for posting preparation.

articles.title: 11. OTP — Okay to Post
articles.body:
This stage means the file is approved and cleared for the bond to be posted.

This may include:

- Final review completed
- Required payment confirmed
- Required cosigner documents completed
- Required approvals completed
- Bond paperwork ready
- Posting team authorized to move forward

Main owner: Manager / Posting Team / Court Team

Goal: Confirm the file is officially ready to post.

articles.title: 12. Defendant Application Needed
articles.body:
This stage is used after the bond is posted or when the defendant still needs to complete their part of the paperwork.

This is important because the cosigner process happens before approval, but the defendant application and defendant documents may happen after release.

This may include:

- Defendant application needed
- Defendant documents needed
- Defendant ID needed
- Defendant contact information needed
- Defendant employment information needed
- Bond condition acknowledgment needed
- Court date acknowledgment needed
- Check-in requirements explained
- Post-release obligations explained

Main owner: Post-Release Team / Court Team / Customer Care

Goal: Get the defendant fully documented and onboarded after release.

categories.title: On Bond & Closeout
categories.desc: Ongoing bond management through final case closeout or refund.

articles.title: 13. ONBND — On Bond
articles.body:
This stage means the defendant is officially on bond.

This is the transition from sales/posting into ongoing case management, court reminders, check-ins, payment monitoring, and compliance.

This may include:

- Bond posted
- Defendant released
- Defendant on bond
- Court reminders active
- Check-in requirements active
- Payment plan active, if applicable
- Bond conditions active
- Ongoing monitoring begins

Main owner: Court Team / Operations / Compliance / Collections, depending on issue

Goal: Manage the customer and defendant through the life of the bond.

articles.title: 14. CLOSD — Closed
articles.body:
This stage means the case or lead is closed.

This may apply when:

- The case is complete
- Bond is discharged
- Bond is exonerated
- Lead is closed
- Customer did not move forward
- File is no longer active
- Obligations are resolved

Main owner: Operations / Court Team / Management

Goal: Close the file cleanly and make sure final status is documented.

articles.title: 15. REFUN — Refund
articles.body:
This stage is used when a refund is needed or being processed.

This may apply when:

- Bond was not approved
- Customer paid but did not move forward
- Overpayment occurred
- Refund is required by company policy
- Case closed with refund due
- Payment needs accounting review

Main owner: Accounting / Manager / Operations

Goal: Track and complete the refund process correctly.

categories.title: Reference
categories.desc: The full pipeline in one place, standard and online paths.

articles.title: Clean Pipeline View
articles.body:
## Standard Process

Intake → JINFO → NYA/NFB → FUP → APPSC → CSAPS → MGRW → Underwriting Review → NEEDS → OLAPP/APRVD → OTP → Defendant Application Needed → ONBND → CLOSD / REFUN

## Online Process

For online leads, the online stages replace the standard cosigner application step.

Intake → JINFO → NYA/NFB → FUP → APPSC → OLPAY → OLSGN/OLSGN1/OLSGN2/OLSGN3 → MGRW → Underwriting Review → NEEDS → OLAPP/APRVD → OTP → Defendant Application Needed → ONBND → CLOSD / REFUN

## Simple Explanation

The lead starts in intake and jail information. If the defendant is not ready to bond, the lead may sit in NYA/NFB or FUP until the case is ready. Once the customer commits, they may move to APPSC and then either the standard CSAPS cosigner application path or the online OLPAY/OLSGN path.

After the application, payment, and cosigner documents are complete, the file moves to MGRW, then Underwriting Review. If anything is missing, it moves to NEEDS. If approved, it moves to OLAPP/APRVD, then OTP when it is okay to post.

After posting and release, Cowboy still needs the Defendant Application, then the case moves to ONBND for ongoing bond management. Final outcomes are CLOSD or REFUN depending on the result.
